era_context: The late 15th century: the Italian Renaissance in full flourish; the Ottoman Empire consolidating control over the eastern Mediterranean; the Iberian Peninsula in the final stages of the Reconquista; China under the Ming Dynasty (Hongwu and Yongle successors); the Aztec Empire at its territorial apex under Ahuitzotl; European exploration accelerating toward the Age of Discovery.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; indigenous records sparse in surviving sources)
Interior Southeast Asia (limited European contact; local chronicles underrepresented in Western archives)
Pacific Islands (no written records; oral traditions not systematically recorded until centuries later)

events.title: Treaty of Medina del Campo between Spain and England
events.description: Spain (Ferdinand and Isabella) and England (Henry VII) signed a treaty establishing mutual defense and trade agreements, including provisions for the marriage of Prince Arthur of Wales to Catherine of Aragon. This alliance consolidated English-Spanish relations and marked a shift in European power dynamics away from France. The treaty was a cornerstone of Henry VII's foreign policy and had lasting consequences for English succession and European alliances.

sources.name: Treaty of Medina del Campo (primary text)

events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-documented in contemporary diplomatic records and has been extensively studied by modern historians.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War (Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War) concludes with Treaty of Constantinople
events.description: The Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War, which began in 1479, formally concluded with the Treaty of Constantinople in January 1489. Venice ceded further territories in the Aegean and Ionian Seas to the Ottoman Empire, including the island of Cyprus (formally transferred in 1489). This marked a decisive shift in Mediterranean naval power toward the Ottomans and signaled the decline of Venetian maritime dominance in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Venetian state archives and Ottoman records

events.certainty_note: The treaty and its terms are well-documented in both Venetian and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Bartolomeu Dias returns to Portugal after rounding the Cape of Good Hope
events.description: Portuguese explorer Bartolomeu Dias returned to Lisbon after successfully rounding the southern tip of Africa (Cape of Good Hope) in 1488, arriving back in December 1488 or early 1489. This voyage proved that a sea route to India was possible, fundamentally altering European understanding of global geography and opening the path for Vasco da Gama's later voyage. The achievement was celebrated by King John II and marked a turning point in Portuguese maritime expansion.

sources.name: Diário da viagem de Bartolomeu Dias (contemporary account, though compiled later)
Pêro de Alenquer, contemporary Portuguese chronicler

events.certainty_note: The voyage and return are well-documented in Portuguese royal records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet operations in the Indian Ocean
events.description: Admiral Zheng He continued his series of massive maritime expeditions (the Treasure Voyages) under the Ming Dynasty, exploring and establishing tributary relationships across the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and Southeast Asia. These voyages, which began in 1405, represented the most advanced naval technology and organization of the era. By 1489, Zheng He's expeditions had already fundamentally mapped the Indian Ocean and established Chinese diplomatic and trade networks across Asia and Africa.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming Dynasty patron, deceased 1424; continued under successors)

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The broader Treasure Voyage program is well-documented; specific activities in 1489 are less precisely dated but consistent with the documented expedition schedule.

events.title: Ahuitzotl's reign continues; Aztec Empire at territorial maximum
events.description: Ahuitzotl (r. 1486–1502) continued his aggressive military campaigns, expanding the Aztec Empire to its greatest territorial extent. By 1489, the empire controlled much of central Mexico through a combination of conquest and tributary relationships. Ahuitzotl was known for his military prowess and for major construction projects in Tenochtitlan, including the expansion of the Templo Mayor. His reign represented the apex of Aztec power before the arrival of Spanish conquistadors.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (compiled post-conquest but based on pre-Columbian sources)
Sahagún, Florentine Codex (16th-century compilation of Nahua accounts)

events.certainty_note: Ahuitzotl's reign and general military activities are well-attested; specific events in 1489 are less precisely dated due to reliance on post-conquest sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Treaty of Medina del Campo is sometimes overstated in older historiography as a decisive moment in European power politics; modern scholarship emphasizes that it was one of many shifting alliances and that its long-term impact was contingent on subsequent events (particularly the death of Prince Arthur in 1502). The Treasure Voyages of Zheng He are sometimes romanticized in popular history as evidence of Chinese 'discovery' of the world; modern scholarship emphasizes that these were diplomatic and tributary missions, not voyages of exploration in the European sense, and that they did not result in sustained Chinese colonial expansion or settlement. The dating of specific Aztec military campaigns under Ahuitzotl is uncertain due to the post-conquest nature of surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1489 is rich for Europe (particularly Spain, Portugal, and England) due to extensive diplomatic archives and contemporary chronicles. Asian sources (Ming China, Ottoman Empire) are also well-preserved. Mesoamerican history relies heavily on post-conquest compilations and codices, which introduce chronological uncertainty and potential Spanish bias. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Pacific remain poorly documented for this year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is less well-attested.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Medina del Campo established the marriage alliance between Arthur and Catherine of Aragon
Continuation of Ottoman expansion that began with the fall of Constantinople
Dias's voyage proved the feasibility of the sea route to India, enabling Vasco da Gama's expedition
Continuation of the Treasure Voyages program initiated under Yongle
Ahuitzotl's expansion of Aztec power set the stage for the empire's confrontation with Spanish conquistadors
